content.text: Git branching lets multiple lines of work coexist safely in one repository. In agentic AI projects — where prompt changes, tool updates, and infrastructure modifications may progress simultaneously — branching prevents one half-finished change from breaking the other. Merging reunites those lines in a controlled way. Understanding both operations deeply means you can choose the right strategy (merge commit, squash, or rebase), resolve conflicts confidently, and maintain a history that is useful for debugging and auditing.

A branch in Git is a lightweight movable pointer to a commit. Creating a branch takes nanoseconds because Git stores only the pointer, not a copy of the repository. When you commit on a branch, the pointer advances;
stays where it was. The two lines of history diverge until you merge them.

Merging integrates one branch into another. Git's merge machinery performs a three-way merge: it finds the common ancestor of the two branch tips and applies the changes from each side. If the same lines of a file changed on both sides, Git reports a conflict and halts, leaving conflict markers for you to resolve. If the branches never touched the same lines, Git merges automatically.

Three merge strategies exist in everyday practice: a regular merge commit (preserves full history with a visible merge node), squash merge (collapses all feature-branch commits into one clean commit on the target branch), and rebase (replays feature-branch commits on top of the target, creating a linear history). Each has different implications for auditing, bisection, and collaboration.

Agent codebases are especially sensitive to half-complete changes. A prompt edit that lands while the retrieval tool is also in flux can produce hard-to-diagnose model behaviour. Branching keeps each work item isolated until it is complete, reviewed, and ready. The commit history on main is then a clean record of discrete, meaningful changes — which is critical for bisecting regressions in agent performance.

For agentic AI specifically, branches serve additional roles: experiment branches isolate prompt A/B tests, hotfix branches let you ship a safety guard without waiting for a long-running feature, and release branches let you maintain a stable production baseline while development continues on main.
work on prompt changes, tool updates, and infrastructure independently without interference.
multiple team members develop simultaneously on separate branches.
a branch that proves harmful can be abandoned without affecting main.
the merged history records what changed and when, satisfying change-management requirements.
prompt or eval experiments in branches never risk production stability.
long-lived branches that diverge from main accumulate conflicts that become expensive to resolve.
switching branches mid-task is fast but can lose uncommitted work if not stashed.
noisy; squash merge trades history clarity for log cleanliness.
rebasing shared branches disrupts teammates; only rebase local branches.
stale branches accumulate; teams need discipline to delete merged branches.

# Create and switch in one step (preferred)
git switch -c feat/rag-v2

# Equivalent older syntax
git checkout -b feat/rag-v2

# List branches (local and remote)
git branch -a

# Show which branch each contributor is on
git branch -v

# Delete a merged branch
git branch -d feat/rag-v2

# Force-delete an unmerged branch (use with caution)
git branch -D feat/rag-v2-abandoned
Consistent naming makes it clear what each branch contains and who owns it:

# Start from a fresh main
git switch main
git pull --ff-only origin main   # fast-forward only; error if diverged

# Branch
git switch -c feat/rag-v2

# Make changes and commit atomically
git add src/rag/retriever.py src/rag/prompt.py
git commit -m "feat(rag): increase chunk size to 512 for better recall

Eval: precision@5 +3% on golden set (37/40 -> 38/40)
Issue: #89"

git add src/rag/reranker.py
git commit -m "feat(rag): add BM25 rerank step post-retrieval"

# Push branch and open PR
git push -u origin feat/rag-v2
gh pr create --title "feat(rag): RAG v2 with chunking and rerank" \
    --body "Eval summary: p@5 improved 3%. No regression on safety golden set."

# After PR approval: merge via GitHub UI or CLI
gh pr merge --squash   # squash all commits into one clean main commit

# Strategy 1: merge commit (preserves full branch history)
git switch main
git merge feat/rag-v2
# Creates: M1 = merge(C3, F3) on main

# Strategy 2: squash merge (F1+F2+F3 -> one commit on main)
git switch main
git merge --squash feat/rag-v2
git commit -m "feat(rag): RAG v2 with chunking and rerank (squash)"
# Use: when feature commits are messy WIP; clean main history

# Strategy 3: fast-forward (only possible if main hasn't moved)
git switch main
git merge --ff-only feat/rag-v2
# Use: short-lived solo branches; keeps linear history without a merge node

# Rebase (linearise branch history BEFORE merging via PR)
git switch feat/rag-v2
git rebase main        # replay F1, F2, F3 on top of current main
git push --force-with-lease origin feat/rag-v2  # update PR branch
# Then merge via PR with --ff-only

attrs.alt: Git branching diagram showing main branch (C1→C2→C3→M1 merge commit) with feat/rag-v2 branching off C2 (F1: update chunk size, F2: rewrite RAG prompt, F3: add rerank step) merging to M1, and hotfix/safety branching off C3 (H1: guard tool call) also merging to M1. Bottom panel shows three merge strategies: merge commit, squash merge, and rebase.

content.text: The diagram shows two common branching patterns. A feature branch (
) branches from an older commit and accumulates several changes before merging back. A hotfix branch (
) branches from the latest main commit, adds one urgent fix, and merges immediately. Both converge at the merge commit M1 on main. The merge strategy panel shows when each approach is appropriate.
regardless of size — even a one-line fix should go through a branch if the project uses pull requests.
merge within days, not weeks, to minimise conflict risk.
branch from the same commit that is currently deployed in production (or the release tag).
should never be merged to main unless the experiment is adopted; delete when done.
when the branch has many WIP commits ("fix typo", "temp debug") that would pollute main history.
only on branches you own locally and have not shared; never rebase a branch a colleague has checked out.

# Scenario: both main and feat/rag-v2 modified src/rag/prompt.py
git switch main
git merge feat/rag-v2
# AUTO-MERGING FAILED

# Inspect conflicts
git status
git diff --diff-filter=U   # show only conflicted files

# Conflict markers in the file:
# <<<<<<< HEAD
# SYSTEM_PROMPT = "You are a RAG assistant. Keep answers concise."
# =======
# SYSTEM_PROMPT = "You are a RAG assistant. Cite your sources."
# >>>>>>> feat/rag-v2

# Edit file to the desired resolution, then:
git add src/rag/prompt.py
git commit   # completes the merge; default message is fine

# Abort a merge and return to pre-merge state
git merge --abort

# Squash WIP commits before opening PR
git switch feat/rag-v2
git rebase -i main    # opens editor listing commits since branch point

# In the editor, mark commits:
# pick  abc1234  feat(rag): increase chunk size
# squash def5678  fix typo in comment
# squash ghi9012  WIP: testing reranker
# reword jkl3456  feat(rag): add BM25 rerank step

# After save: Git replays commits, combining squashed ones
# and prompting for commit messages

# Force-push the cleaned branch
git push --force-with-lease origin feat/rag-v2
# --force-with-lease: fails if remote has new commits (safer than --force)
Stashing uncommitted work before switching branches

# Save uncommitted changes without committing
git stash push -m "WIP: adjusting temperature parameter"

# Switch to fix something urgent
git switch hotfix/safety
# ... make fix, commit, push, switch back ...

git switch feat/rag-v2

# Restore stashed work
git stash pop

# List all stashes
git stash list
# stash@{0}: On feat/rag-v2: WIP: adjusting temperature parameter

# Apply a specific stash without removing it
git stash apply stash@{0}

# Discard top stash
git stash drop

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Day's work: experiment with a new retrieval strategy

# Start clean
git switch main && git pull --ff-only origin main

# Open experiment branch
git switch -c experiment/hyde-retrieval

# Implement HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) in retriever
vim src/rag/retriever.py

# Commit
git add src/rag/retriever.py
git commit -m "experiment(rag): add HyDE retrieval strategy

Hypothetical Document Embeddings: generate a hypothetical answer,
embed it, then retrieve against the real corpus.
Eval running in CI — will compare to baseline BM25 + rerank."

# Push and open draft PR for visibility
git push -u origin experiment/hyde-retrieval
gh pr create --draft \
    --title "[experiment] HyDE retrieval" \
    --body "Draft: eval results pending. See eval/hyde_vs_baseline.json when CI completes."

# Meanwhile main advances (colleague merged a hotfix)
git fetch origin
git rebase origin/main  # keep experiment branch current

# After eval: if results positive, mark PR ready for review
gh pr ready

# Commits on feat/rag-v2 not yet on main
git log main..feat/rag-v2 --oneline

# What main has that feat/rag-v2 doesn't (since branch point)
git log feat/rag-v2..main --oneline

# Full diff of everything on the branch
git diff main...feat/rag-v2    # three dots: diff from common ancestor

# Visualise branch graph in terminal
git log --oneline --graph --all --decorate | head -30

A three-person agent team uses a trunk-based development model with short-lived branches. The prompt engineer, backend developer, and DevOps engineer each work on a branch simultaneously; all merge to main within 48 hours via squash merge through PR.

# Prompt engineer
git switch -c feat/system-prompt-v3
# edits prompts/system.txt
git commit -m "feat(prompt): tighten citation instruction in system prompt

Eval: factual precision +2%, hallucination rate -1.5% on eval-set-2024-Q4"
gh pr create --title "feat(prompt): system prompt v3" --body "..."

# Backend developer (same time)
git switch -c feat/parallel-tool-calls
# implements parallel tool execution
git commit -m "feat(tools): execute independent tools in parallel"
gh pr create --title "feat(tools): parallel tool execution"

# DevOps (same time)
git switch -c chore/bump-anthropic-sdk
pip install --upgrade anthropic
pip freeze | grep anthropic >> requirements.txt
git commit -am "chore: upgrade anthropic SDK to 0.38.0"
gh pr create --title "chore: upgrade anthropic SDK"

# All three PRs reviewed, CI passes, merged to main via squash
# main now has three clean commits, one per unit of work
if a colleague has checked out your branch, rebasing rewrites its history and breaks their local copy. Only rebase branches you alone are working on.
a branch open for weeks accumulates divergence and leads to painful conflicts. Merge partial work behind a feature flag instead.
bypasses review and CI; protect main with a branch protection rule requiring PR approval.
a branch that changes the prompt, updates a dependency, and refactors a module is impossible to review or revert cleanly. One concern per branch.
overwrites any commits pushed since your last fetch;
fails safely if the remote has advanced.
Keep branches short-lived — aim for less than 48 hours from branch creation to merge PR.
Write commit messages that explain why, not just what: 'Reduce timeout to prevent hung agent loops' rather than 'Edit timeout value'.
Use squash merge for feature branches with WIP commits; use merge commits for release branches where you want to preserve the full history.
Set up branch protection rules on main: require PR review, require CI to pass, disable direct push.
Delete merged branches immediately — stale branches are noise and can mislead teammates.

Git is designed for large repositories. Creating a branch is O(1) — it writes a single 41-byte file containing the commit SHA. Merging scales with the size of the diff, not the repository size. For very large monorepos, consider sparse checkouts (
) to only materialise the subdirectory you are working in.

# Sparse checkout: only materialise src/rag/ and prompts/
git sparse-checkout init --cone
git sparse-checkout set src/rag prompts

# Check what is currently sparse-checked-out
git sparse-checkout list

# Return to full checkout
git sparse-checkout disable
PR/MR workflows wrap branching with review, CI, and merge policies; support squash, rebase, and merge commit strategies per-repository.
for branch operations since Git 2.23; clearer semantics for creating and switching branches.
check out multiple branches simultaneously in separate directories — useful for running evals on two branches side-by-side without stashing.
GitHub's CLI for creating PRs, listing branches, and managing review from the terminal.
, etc.) that enables automated changelogs and semantic versioning from Git history.

Branch protection rules are the primary access-control mechanism for a Git repository's main branch. Require: at least one approving PR review, all required CI checks passing, branch up to date with main before merge, and signed commits (for audit trails). These rules prevent unapproved prompt or tool changes from reaching production.
For agentic AI systems that operate in production, the history of the prompts/ and tools/ directories is a security artefact. Tag releases (
) with GPG-signed tags so the exact agent configuration at any point in time is verifiable. Store eval results alongside commits using Git notes or a linked artefact system.

Think of Git branches as parallel timelines, and merging as the moment two timelines converge. Each timeline (branch) records a complete, self-consistent history of changes. When timelines converge, Git automatically resolves non-overlapping changes; you resolve the overlapping ones manually. The choice of merge strategy determines how the converged history looks — branchy graph (merge commit), flat line (squash/rebase) — and whether future
A branch is a cheap pointer to a commit; creating one is instant and costs no disk space.
Merge strategies: merge commit (preserves history), squash (clean main), rebase (linear history, local branches only).
Keep branches short-lived (less than 48 hours) to minimise conflict risk.
One concern per branch: prompt change, tool update, and dependency bump should be separate branches and PRs.
Never rebase branches that are shared with teammates — only rebase local branches.
Protect main with branch protection rules: require PR, require CI, block direct push.
when updating remote branches after a rebase.
